title: Names Run In Two Directions
statement:
Empty editor. Build the table shown in the target on the right.

Four currencies, each with a buy rate and a sell rate.

The question from lesson 53 does all the work: take a cell from the middle and say what it means.

1.94 — that's the buy rate for the euro. Notice that saying it took TWO things: what's above and what's to the left.

So the names aren't only in the first row.
constraints: The table has a name and a date
Euro 1.94 and 1.98 · Dollar 1.72 and 1.76 · Pound 2.21 and 2.27 · Franc 2.03 and 2.09
Every cell that names something is a header cell — whichever way it points

checks.t3: There are at least five rows
checks.t4: There are eight cells with numbers
checks.t6: Every row starts with a header cell
checks.t9: The name is inside the table
checks.t12: There is a sentence below it

why.empty: The editor is empty. There is no starter code here — you build the table yourself.
why.not-closed:
Some tag is opened and never closed.

A table is the usual place: every row and every cell wants closing, and there are a lot of them. And be careful not to mix them — a header cell closes with its own tag, not with the ordinary one.
why.few-rows: There aren't five rows. Four currencies make four rows, and above them sits the row with the column names.
why.few-cells: There aren't eight cells with numbers. Four currencies times two numbers each makes eight.
why.few-th:
There aren't seven header cells.

Three along the top — Currency, Buy, Sell. And one at the start of each of the four rows — the currency's name.

Euro isn't a value. It isn't like 1.94. It's the NAME of the row.
why.no-row-names:
The rows don't start with a header cell.

That's the trap: you put names along the top and stopped. On screen it looks perfect — the browser only emboldens what you told it to anyway.

But take the cell 1.72 and name it. To say "the buy rate for the dollar" you need two things: the column above and the currency to the left.

Without the second, a blind visitor hears only: Buy, 1.72. Buy what?
why.empty-th: There is an empty header cell. An empty name names nothing — the screen reader will just say "blank".
why.no-caption:
The table has no name, or it is empty.

The page heading doesn't do this job — it belongs to the page, not to the table.

And here the name carries something else: rates go stale. Without a date the table doesn't say when it was true.
why.caption-outside:
The table's name isn't inside it.

Its place is the first thing after the opening tag, before the first row. Not above the table, but in it.
why.no-heading: There is no main heading, or it is empty.
why.no-text: There is no sentence below the heading, or it is empty.

no-row-names: Take any cell with a number and say it out loud: what is this?
To name it you need two things — the column and the row. So both of them name something.
Euro, Dollar, Pound, Franc aren't values. They're row names.
few-th: Count how many things in this table are NAMES rather than values.
Three along the top and one at the start of each row.
Careful: change a cell's tag and change its closing tag too.
no-caption: The table has a name of its own, separate from the page heading.
Its place is INSIDE the table — the first thing, before the first row.
Say what it holds and from when. Tomorrow the rates are different.

solution.why:
The first instinct is to put the column names along the top and get on with the data. That's how most tables are built and it looks finished.

But look at what's left: the cell 2.21 sits in the Buy column and in a row that isn't called anything. Formally it has an address; in practice it has half an address.

For you that's no problem — you see the whole table at once and your eyes drift left on their own to find the currency. A blind visitor hears the cells one at a time. Reaching 2.21, their computer says: Buy, 2.21. Buy what?

A header cell doesn't mean "the first row". It means "this isn't a value, this is a name". And here the names run in two directions — down and across.
steps: Start with the heading and the sentence below it.
Open the table and give it a name right away — the first thing inside, before the rows.
The first row is the column names. All three are names, not values.
Then come the four currencies, one row each.
Now the important part: the first cell of every row. Is it a value, or a name?
It's a name. So it wants the same tag as the ones along the top.
Finally take a cell from the middle and name it out loud. Do the two addresses cover it?
solution.code:
Currency rates

Rates against the lev, taken at noon.

Exchange rates, 14 July 2025
Currency Buy Sell
Euro 1.94 1.98
Dollar 1.72 1.76
Pound 2.21 2.27
Franc 2.03 2.09
solution.note:
Load it and look: the currency names come out bold and centred. Pleasant, but that isn't the point.

The point is that every cell now has two addresses. Lift any one of them out of the table and you can still say what it means — and that is precisely the definition of a table.
